text: Tilt the world. Resolve gravity into components along and into a ramp to predict how blocks slide, press, and accelerate.

pretrieval.displayText: Before the lesson, make a hunch: as a ramp gets steeper, what happens to gravity's pull along the ramp?
pretrieval.explanation: The lesson will split gravity into along-ramp and into-ramp components, then use those pieces to predict motion and forces.
options: The along-ramp pull gets smaller because the block is higher up.
The along-ramp pull stays the same because gravity is constant.
The along-ramp pull gets larger, while the into-ramp pressing component gets smaller.
The normal force gets larger, so the block always slides faster.

steps.displayText: This is a free-body diagram on a ramp. Gravity still points straight down, but we resolve it with the right triangle into a piece along the surface (mg·sinθ, what makes the block slide) and a piece pressing into it (mg·cosθ, balanced by the normal force). Drag the angle slider and press Release: a steeper ramp grows the sliding component, so the block slides faster.

Where does the speed-at-the-bottom formula come from? You already met v² = v₀² + 2aΔx in 1D Kinematics. A block released from rest on the ramp starts at v₀ = 0, accelerates at a = g·sinθ, and slides the ramp length Δx = L, giving v² = 2aL. Release the block and watch the graph: its speed grows with the square root of the distance covered, reaching v = √(2aL) at the bottom. Change the angle to see a steeper ramp arrive faster — and notice the result never depends on the block's mass.

Here are the ramp results in one place. The normal force and along-ramp pull are nothing new: they are just gravity (mg) split with the right triangle, exactly the decomposition from the free-body lesson. Review them before solving the problems that follow.

equations.label: Normal force: the into-ramp slice of gravity (mg resolved with the right triangle)
Along-ramp pull: the down-ramp slice of the same triangle
Speed after sliding L from rest (derived in the previous step)

steps.displayText: A block is released from rest on a frictionless ramp tilted at θ = 30°. (Use g = 9.8 m/s².) Calculate its acceleration down the ramp (m/s²) and enter your answer.
steps.explanation: Only the along-ramp component of gravity accelerates the block: a = g·sinθ = 9.8·sin(30°) = 9.8·0.5 = 4.9 m/s^2.
steps.hint: Only the along-ramp slice of gravity speeds it up: a = g*sin(theta).

steps.displayText: A block of mass m = 2 kg rests on a ramp at θ = 30°. (Use g = 9.8 m/s².) Calculate the normal force the ramp exerts on it (N) and enter your answer.
steps.explanation: The normal force balances the into-ramp component of gravity: N = m·g·cosθ = 2·9.8·cos(30°) = 19.6·0.866 = 17.0 N.
steps.hint: The normal force balances the into-ramp slice of gravity: N = m*g*cos(theta).

steps.displayText: For the same block (m = 2 kg) on the θ = 30° ramp, calculate the component of gravity pulling it along the ramp (N) and enter your answer.
steps.explanation: The along-ramp pull is F = m·g·sinθ = 2·9.8·sin(30°) = 19.6·0.5 = 9.8 N.
steps.hint: The along-ramp pull is F = m*g*sin(theta).

steps.displayText: The block slides from rest down the full L = 10 m of the frictionless θ = 30° ramp. Calculate how fast it is moving at the bottom (m/s) and enter your answer.
steps.explanation: First a = g·sinθ = 4.9 m/s^2. Then v = sqrt(2·a·L) = sqrt(2·4.9·10) = sqrt(98) = 9.9 m/s.
steps.hint: First find a = g*sin(theta), then use v = sqrt(2*a*L) for a start from rest.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a block is released on a frictionless ramp tilted at theta = 45 degrees. Use g = 9.8 m/s^2. Calculate its acceleration down the ramp, rounded to the nearest tenth (m/s^2).
Retrieval practice: a 3 kg block rests on a ramp at theta = 60 degrees. Use g = 9.8 m/s^2. Calculate the normal force, rounded to the nearest newton.
retrieval.explanation: Use N = m*g*cos(theta) = 3*9.8*cos(60 degrees) = 29.4*0.5 = 14.7 N, so about 15 N.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a 4 kg block sits on a theta = 30 degree ramp. Use g = 9.8 m/s^2. Calculate the component of gravity pulling it along the ramp (N).
retrieval.hint: Use F = m*g*sin(theta) for the along-ramp pull.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a block slides from rest down L = 20 m of a frictionless theta = 30 degree ramp. Calculate its speed at the bottom, rounded to the nearest tenth (m/s).
retrieval.explanation: First a = g*sin(30 degrees) = 4.9 m/s^2. Then v = sqrt(2*a*L) = sqrt(2*4.9*20) = sqrt(196) = 14.0 m/s.
retrieval.hint: Find a = g*sin(theta), then v = sqrt(2*a*L).

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a block is released on a frictionless ramp tilted at theta = 15 degrees. Use g = 9.8 m/s^2. Calculate its acceleration down the ramp, rounded to the nearest tenth (m/s^2).
retrieval.hint: Use a = g*sin(theta); a small angle gives a small acceleration.
